_metadata.source_policy: All disease entries cite at least 2 US university extension publications. Chemical recommendations use families (e.g., 'copper-based fungicide') rather than brand names because formulation availability is state-regulated and changes with EPA rulings. Drafted by Claude for Quinn Evans (qevans@student.neumont.edu) review.
_metadata.disclaimer: AI-generated diagnoses are educational. For critical agricultural decisions, consult a qualified agronomist or your local agricultural extension office for region-specific guidance.

Apple___Apple_scab.summary: A fungal disease (Venturia inaequalis) producing olive-green to brown velvety lesions on leaves and corky scabs on fruit.
Apple___Apple_scab.time_sensitivity: Begin protective sprays at green-tip (early spring) and continue every 10–14 days through wet weather. Treat infected trees within 7 days of first symptom to prevent secondary spread.
action_steps: Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn to break the disease's overwintering cycle.
Apply a protectant fungicide (captan, sulfur, or copper-based) starting at green-tip and continuing at 10–14 day intervals during rainy spring weather.
Plant resistant cultivars (Liberty, Enterprise, Pristine, Williams' Pride) for new orchard installations.
Prune the canopy to improve air circulation — wet leaves are infection sites, dry leaves are not.
Inspect weekly during the growing season and remove any infected leaves you can reach.

Apple___Black_rot.summary: A fungal disease (Botryosphaeria obtusa) causing 'frogeye' leaf spots, fruit rot with concentric rings, and limb cankers.
Apple___Black_rot.time_sensitivity: Prune out cankers during dormant season (winter). Begin protective fungicide sprays at petal fall and continue through summer in wet years.
action_steps: Prune out limb cankers and dead wood during dormant pruning; burn or remove debris from the orchard.
Remove mummified fruit from the tree and ground — these are the main inoculum source for next season.
Apply a captan, mancozeb, or thiophanate-methyl spray at petal fall, continuing on a 10–14 day schedule.
Reduce tree stress: maintain consistent irrigation and balanced fertility (excess nitrogen worsens susceptibility).
Sanitize pruning tools with 10% bleach between cuts on infected wood.

sources.name: Missouri Botanical Garden — Apple Black Rot

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.summary: A fungal disease (Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae) that alternates between apples and junipers/cedars; produces orange-yellow leaf spots and fruit lesions.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.time_sensitivity: Protective sprays must be applied during early-to-mid May during spore release from juniper galls. Once leaves are spotted, treatment is largely cosmetic for that season.
action_steps: Remove nearby Eastern red cedar or juniper hosts (within ~1 mile if possible) — these are the alternate host required for the disease's life cycle.
Apply a myclobutanil, propiconazole, or mancozeb fungicide at pink bud and continue through petal fall.
Plant resistant apple cultivars (Liberty, Redfree, Williams' Pride) for new installations.
Accept some cosmetic damage on established trees — defoliation is usually mild and rarely affects fruit yield significantly.
Inspect cedar/juniper hosts in early spring and prune out the orange jelly-like galls before spore release.

sources.name: Cornell Cooperative Extension — Cedar Apple Rust

Apple___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of an unstressed apple tree.
maintenance_tips: Continue regular scouting — many apple diseases (scab, fire blight, rust) appear suddenly during wet weather.
Maintain consistent irrigation; avoid wetting foliage in the evening to reduce overnight leaf wetness.
Apply balanced fertility based on a soil test; excess nitrogen increases disease susceptibility.
Prune annually during dormant season for air circulation and to remove dead/diseased wood early.

sources.name: Penn State Extension — Apple Production Guide

Background_without_leaves.summary: The uploaded image does not appear to contain a single leaf in clear focus. GreenVision is trained to diagnose individual leaves and cannot reliably analyze this image.
retake_guidance: Photograph a single leaf against a plain, contrasting background (a sheet of white paper works well).
Hold the camera 6–12 inches from the leaf so the leaf fills most of the frame.
Ensure the leaf is in focus and well-lit — avoid harsh shadows and direct sunlight glare.
If diagnosing a sick plant, photograph the most affected leaf, not the whole plant.

Blueberry___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of a thriving blueberry plant.
maintenance_tips: Maintain acidic soil pH (4.5–5.2) — most blueberry problems start with the wrong soil chemistry.
Mulch with pine needles or pine bark to keep roots cool and conserve moisture.
Scout monthly for mummy berry disease, anthracnose, and root rot, especially in wet years.
Prune annually during dormant season to remove old canes and improve air circulation.

Cherry___Powdery_mildew.summary: A fungal disease (Podosphaera clandestina) producing white powdery growth on leaves, shoots, and fruit; reduces fruit quality and tree vigor.
Cherry___Powdery_mildew.time_sensitivity: Apply protectant sprays at shuck split and continue every 10–14 days until harvest. Once infection is established, sulfur or oil-based treatments slow but don't reverse damage.
action_steps: Apply a sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, or systemic fungicide (myclobutanil, triflumizole) starting at shuck split.
Prune the canopy to improve air circulation — powdery mildew thrives in stagnant air with high humidity.
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the root zone.
Plant resistant cultivars (Surefire is moderately resistant) for new installations.
Remove and destroy heavily infected shoots during dormant pruning to reduce inoculum.

sources.name: WSU Tree Fruit — Cherry Powdery Mildew
UC IPM — Powdery Mildew on Stone Fruit

Cherry___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of an unstressed cherry tree.
maintenance_tips: Scout regularly for brown rot, leaf spot, and powdery mildew — cherry trees are sensitive to multiple fungal pressures.
Apply dormant copper spray once in late winter to suppress overwintering fungal inoculum.
Prune for an open vase or central leader form to maximize air circulation through the canopy.
Avoid wounding bark during the growing season — bacterial canker enters through wounds.

Corn___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_sp.summary: A fungal disease (Cercospora zeae-maydis) producing rectangular tan-to-gray lesions parallel to leaf veins; can cause significant yield loss in continuous-corn systems.
Corn___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_sp.time_sensitivity: Scout from V8 stage onward; apply fungicide between VT (tasseling) and R2 (blister) if disease is reaching the ear leaf, particularly in susceptible hybrids.
action_steps: Rotate out of corn for at least one year to break the disease cycle (the fungus overwinters in corn residue).
Till crop residue or use a clean-tilled field for the following corn crop where conservation practices allow.
Apply a foliar fungicide (azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin, or propiconazole) between VT and R2 if scouting shows lesions reaching the ear leaf.
Plant hybrids rated for gray leaf spot resistance in fields with a history of the disease.
Balance nitrogen fertility — both deficiency and excess increase susceptibility.

sources.name: Iowa State Extension — Gray Leaf Spot
Penn State Extension — Gray Leaf Spot

Corn___Common_rust.summary: A fungal disease (Puccinia sorghi) producing small, round, reddish-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces; rarely economically significant in field corn but can damage sweet corn.
Corn___Common_rust.time_sensitivity: Most years no treatment is needed in field corn. In sweet corn or seed corn, apply fungicide if pustules appear before tasseling and weather favors continued infection.
action_steps: Scout from V6 onward — common rust typically arrives on wind currents from southern states.
Plant resistant hybrids for sweet corn production where common rust is regularly problematic.
Apply a triazole or strobilurin fungicide (propiconazole, azoxystrobin) only if scouting shows pustules on the ear leaf before tasseling.
Accept low pustule loads in field corn — yield impact is usually negligible.
Time planting to avoid peak rust arrival if local extension offices issue rust alerts.

Corn___Northern_Leaf_Blight.summary: A fungal disease (Exserohilum turcicum) producing long cigar-shaped grayish lesions on leaves; can cause severe yield loss when infection reaches the ear leaf before silking.
Corn___Northern_Leaf_Blight.time_sensitivity: Scout from V8 onward; apply foliar fungicide between V10 and VT if lesions reach 4 leaves below the ear leaf.
action_steps: Plant hybrids with the Ht1, Ht2, or HtN resistance gene appropriate to your local race of the pathogen.
Rotate corn with a non-host crop (soybeans, small grains) for at least one year.
Till crop residue or rotate to break the disease's overwintering cycle in corn stubble.
Apply a foliar fungicide (azoxystrobin + propiconazole or similar combo) between V10 and VT when lesions are advancing.
Use clean seed and rotate fungicide modes of action across years to delay resistance.

sources.name: Iowa State Extension — Northern Corn Leaf Blight
Crop Protection Network — Northern Corn Leaf Blight

Corn___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of well-managed corn.
maintenance_tips: Scout fields weekly from V6 through R3 for gray leaf spot, northern leaf blight, and common rust.
Maintain balanced fertility based on soil tests; both deficiency and excess nitrogen increase disease pressure.
Rotate to a non-host crop (soybeans, small grains) at least once every 2–3 years.
Choose hybrids with resistance to diseases your fields have shown in past years.

Grape___Black_rot.summary: A fungal disease (Guignardia bidwellii) producing tan-brown leaf spots and black mummified fruit; can destroy an entire crop in a wet year if untreated.
Grape___Black_rot.time_sensitivity: Begin protectant sprays at 3–5 inch shoot growth and continue every 10–14 days through bloom and early fruit set. Once mummies form, that fruit is lost for the season.
action_steps: Remove all mummified berries from the canopy and ground during dormant season — these are the main inoculum source.
Apply a protectant fungicide (mancozeb, captan, or ziram) starting at 3–5 inch shoot growth, continuing every 10–14 days through bloom.
Prune for an open canopy to improve air circulation and shorten leaf wetness duration.
Plant resistant cultivars (most American hybrids) in regions with consistent black rot pressure.
Apply systemic fungicides (myclobutanil, tebuconazole) during the pre-bloom and bloom periods for highest efficacy.

sources.name: Ohio State Extension — Black Rot of Grape

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).summary: A complex of fungal trunk diseases producing 'tiger-stripe' leaf patterns, dark spots on fruit, and progressive vine decline; no curative treatment is available.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).time_sensitivity: Manage during winter pruning. Sick vines cannot be cured — focus is on slowing spread to healthy vines and replanting affected blocks over time.
action_steps: Identify symptomatic vines during the growing season and mark them for either remedial trunk surgery or removal.
Practice late dormant pruning (delaying cuts) and protect large pruning wounds with a fungicide paste (thiophanate-methyl + boric acid).
Avoid pruning during wet weather — spores enter through fresh wounds.
Consider trunk renewal (cutting back to a sucker from the base) on moderately affected vines to extend their productive life.
Plant replacement vines from a clean nursery source; avoid replanting affected vines into infected soil immediately.

sources.name: UC IPM — Esca and Petri Disease

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.summary: A fungal disease (Pseudocercospora vitis) causing irregular dark-brown leaf lesions; severe infections defoliate vines and reduce fruit ripening.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.time_sensitivity: Begin protectant sprays at fruit set if conditions are humid; continue every 10–14 days through veraison in years of high disease pressure.
action_steps: Remove fallen leaves and prunings from the vineyard floor during dormant season to reduce overwintering inoculum.
Apply a protectant fungicide (mancozeb or captan) starting at fruit set during humid years.
Open the canopy through summer leaf-pulling and shoot positioning to dry leaves faster after rain.
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization — dense canopies hold humidity and encourage infection.
Inspect weekly during the growing season; spot-treat localized outbreaks before they spread.

sources.name: Cornell Cooperative Extension — Grape Leaf Diseases

Grape___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of an unstressed grapevine.
maintenance_tips: Scout weekly from bud break through harvest for the four major fungal diseases (black rot, downy mildew, powdery mildew, Botrytis).
Apply a dormant copper spray and protectant fungicides on a calendar schedule appropriate to your region's disease pressure.
Maintain canopy openness through shoot positioning and leaf pulling to dry leaves faster after rain.
Test soil periodically; balanced potassium and phosphorus support natural disease resistance.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).summary: A bacterial disease (Candidatus Liberibacter spp.) spread by the Asian citrus psyllid; causes blotchy mottled leaves, lopsided bitter fruit, and progressive tree death. There is no cure.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).time_sensitivity: Act immediately on first suspicion. Confirmed infected trees should be removed promptly to protect neighbors. Psyllid vector control should run continuously.
action_steps: Submit symptomatic leaves to your state's plant diagnostic lab for PCR confirmation — visual diagnosis is unreliable for early infection.
Remove and destroy confirmed infected trees immediately; the disease is incurable and spreads to neighbors via the psyllid vector.
Manage Asian citrus psyllid populations with foliar insecticides and biological controls under guidance from your local extension office.
Plant only certified disease-free nursery stock; avoid moving plant material from regulated areas.
Report suspected cases to USDA APHIS or your state Department of Agriculture — this is a regulated disease in most citrus-growing states.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.summary: A bacterial disease (Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni) producing small angular leaf spots and pitted fruit lesions; severity ranges from cosmetic to defoliating depending on cultivar and weather.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.time_sensitivity: Apply copper sprays during dormant and bud-swell stages; continue with reduced-rate copper or oxytetracycline through the growing season in high-pressure years.
action_steps: Plant resistant or moderately resistant cultivars (Contender, Encore, Bounty) for new orchard installations.
Apply a dormant copper spray at leaf fall and again at bud swell — this is the highest-leverage timing.
Use lower-rate copper or oxytetracycline sprays during the growing season; full-rate copper after bloom causes phytotoxicity (leaf damage).
Avoid overhead irrigation and wet-soil disturbance that splashes bacteria onto leaves.
Maintain balanced fertility; excess nitrogen produces lush growth that's more susceptible.

sources.name: Penn State Extension — Peach Bacterial Spot

Peach___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of a well-managed peach tree.
maintenance_tips: Scout weekly during the growing season for peach leaf curl, bacterial spot, and brown rot.
Apply a single dormant fungicide spray (chlorothalonil or copper) before bud swell — this single application controls most peach leaf curl.
Thin fruit aggressively (one fruit per 6 inches of branch) — overloaded trees stress and become more disease-susceptible.
Prune annually to maintain an open vase shape; air circulation through the canopy is the single best non-chemical disease management tool.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.summary: A bacterial disease (Xanthomonas spp.) producing small water-soaked leaf spots that dry to dark brown; severe infections cause defoliation, fruit lesions, and unmarketable yield.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.time_sensitivity: Begin copper sprays at first symptom; continue every 7–10 days during wet weather. Once severe infection is established, treatment slows but does not reverse damage.
action_steps: Use certified pathogen-free seed and transplants from reputable suppliers — most outbreaks originate from infected planting stock.
Apply copper-based bactericides (often tank-mixed with mancozeb) starting at first symptom; reapply every 7–10 days in wet weather.
Rotate to a non-host crop (corn, beans, lettuce) for at least 2 years after a severe outbreak.
Avoid overhead irrigation; switch to drip irrigation to reduce leaf wetness.
Remove and destroy crop residue immediately after harvest — the bacterium overwinters on plant debris.

sources.name: UMN Extension — Bacterial Spot of Pepper

Pepper,_bell___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of an unstressed pepper plant.
maintenance_tips: Use drip irrigation and mulch to keep leaves dry — most pepper diseases (bacterial spot, anthracnose, Phytophthora) need leaf wetness to spread.
Rotate pepper out of beds where peppers, tomatoes, or eggplants grew in the previous 2–3 years.
Scout weekly for aphids and thrips — these are vectors for the major pepper viruses.
Stake or cage tall varieties to keep fruit off the soil where it can pick up soil-borne pathogens.

Potato___Early_blight.summary: A fungal disease (Alternaria solani) producing dark concentric ring spots on older leaves; typically not catastrophic but progressively defoliates the plant and reduces tuber yield.
Potato___Early_blight.time_sensitivity: Apply protectant fungicide when scouting first detects lesions, typically 4–6 weeks after emergence. Continue every 7–14 days through the season in high-pressure years.
action_steps: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes from reputable suppliers.
Apply a protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) when first lesions appear, then every 7–14 days.
Rotate to non-Solanaceae crops (corn, small grains, legumes) for at least 2 years.
Maintain balanced fertility — early blight is more severe on stressed, low-fertility plants.
Hill regularly to keep tubers covered and reduce direct rain splash onto lower leaves.

sources.name: UMN Extension — Early Blight of Potato

Potato___Late_blight.summary: A devastating water mold disease (Phytophthora infestans) that can destroy a field in 7–10 days under cool, wet conditions; the same pathogen caused the Irish Potato Famine.
Potato___Late_blight.time_sensitivity: ACT IMMEDIATELY. Apply systemic fungicide within 24 hours of first lesion. Once an outbreak is established, defensive fungicide is the only path to saving any of the crop.
action_steps: Apply a systemic fungicide (cymoxanil, propamocarb, or fluazinam) at the first sign of lesions — within 24 hours if possible.
Increase scouting frequency to daily once the disease is in the area — a regional 'late blight watch' may be active.
Plant resistant cultivars (Defender, Kennebec, Elba) in regions with regular late blight pressure.
Destroy cull piles and volunteer potatoes immediately — these are the primary overwintering inoculum sources.
If the foliage is destroyed, kill the haulm 2 weeks before harvest to harden tuber skins and prevent tuber infection during digging.

Potato___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of an unstressed potato plant.
maintenance_tips: Plant only certified disease-free seed potatoes from reputable suppliers.
Scout daily during cool wet weather for late blight — this disease can destroy a field in under two weeks.
Hill regularly to keep tubers below the soil surface, reducing direct rain splash and greening.
Maintain consistent irrigation; alternating wet and dry stress increases susceptibility to multiple diseases.

Raspberry___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of an unstressed raspberry plant.
maintenance_tips: Remove spent floricanes immediately after harvest to reduce overwintering disease inoculum.
Scout for cane diseases (anthracnose, spur blight, cane blight) — these enter through wounds and pruning cuts.
Maintain a narrow trellised row to improve air circulation through the canopy.
Test soil pH every 2–3 years; raspberries prefer slightly acidic soil (pH 5.8–6.5).

Soybean___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of well-managed soybeans.
maintenance_tips: Scout fields weekly from V6 through R5 for SDS (sudden death syndrome), white mold, and frogeye leaf spot.
Rotate to non-host crops (corn, small grains) for at least one year between soybean crops.
Plant varieties with appropriate resistance packages for your county's known disease pressures.
Treat seed with appropriate fungicide and insecticide treatments at planting in fields with known disease history.

sources.name: Crop Protection Network — Soybean Disease Guide

Squash___Powdery_mildew.summary: A fungal disease (Podosphaera xanthii) producing white powdery growth on leaves and stems; reduces photosynthesis, yield, and fruit quality, but rarely catastrophic.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.time_sensitivity: Apply protectant sprays starting at first symptom and continue every 7–14 days through harvest. Sulfur and potassium bicarbonate are effective if applied early.
action_steps: Apply a sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, or horticultural oil spray at first symptom; reapply every 7–14 days.
Plant resistant varieties (many newer cucurbit varieties carry powdery mildew resistance) for next year.
Avoid overhead irrigation late in the day — wet leaves at night encourage infection.
Space plants for good air circulation; powdery mildew thrives in stagnant humid air.
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves to reduce inoculum; sanitize tools between uses.

sources.name: UC IPM — Powdery Mildew on Cucurbits

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.summary: A fungal disease (Diplocarpon earliana) producing small purple-to-brown leaf spots that coalesce; severe infections defoliate plants and reduce yield.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.time_sensitivity: Apply protectant fungicide starting at pre-bloom and continuing through fruit set in high-pressure years. Cleanup at renovation is the highest-leverage non-chemical step.
action_steps: Mow plants down to 1 inch immediately after harvest ('renovation') and remove the clippings — this is the single highest-leverage practice.
Apply a protectant fungicide (captan or myclobutanil) starting at pre-bloom in matted-row systems with a history of leaf scorch.
Use drip irrigation and mulch with straw to keep leaves dry.
Plant resistant varieties (Earliglow, Allstar) where leaf scorch is a recurring issue.
Maintain narrow rows through hoeing to improve air circulation.

sources.name: Penn State Extension — Strawberry Leaf Diseases

Strawberry___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of an unstressed strawberry plant.
maintenance_tips: Mulch with straw to keep berries off bare soil and reduce gray mold pressure.
Renovate matted-row plantings immediately after harvest — mow plants to 1 inch and remove debris.
Scout weekly for leaf diseases (scorch, leaf spot, leaf blight) and spider mites.
Use drip irrigation, not overhead, to minimize leaf wetness duration.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.summary: A bacterial disease (Xanthomonas spp.) producing small dark water-soaked leaf spots and scabby fruit lesions; severe infections cause defoliation and unmarketable fruit.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.time_sensitivity: Begin copper sprays at first symptom; continue every 7–10 days through wet weather. Acibenzolar-S-methyl (ASM, e.g., Actigard) applied preventively reduces severity if started before symptoms.
action_steps: Use certified pathogen-free seed and transplants — most outbreaks originate from infected planting stock.
Apply copper-based bactericides (often tank-mixed with mancozeb) at first symptom; repeat every 7–10 days during wet weather.
Apply acibenzolar-S-methyl (ASM, Actigard) preventively starting at transplant for highest-leverage suppression in high-pressure regions.
Rotate to a non-Solanaceae crop for at least 2 years after severe outbreaks.
Avoid working in plants when foliage is wet — bacteria spread on hands, tools, and clothing.

sources.name: UMN Extension — Bacterial Spot of Tomato
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Tomato Bacterial Spot

Tomato___Early_blight.summary: A fungal disease (Alternaria solani) producing dark concentric ring spots on older leaves; progressive defoliation reduces fruit yield and exposes fruit to sun scald.
Tomato___Early_blight.time_sensitivity: Apply protectant fungicide when first lesions appear on lower leaves, typically 4–6 weeks after transplant. Continue every 7–14 days.
action_steps: Apply a protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) at first symptom and continue every 7–14 days through harvest.
Mulch heavily to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves — early blight overwinters in soil.
Prune lower leaves to reduce moisture and remove the most-susceptible foliage.
Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground.
Rotate to a non-Solanaceae crop for at least 2 years.

sources.name: UMN Extension — Early Blight of Tomato

Tomato___Late_blight.summary: A devastating water mold disease (Phytophthora infestans) producing rapidly expanding water-soaked lesions; can destroy a tomato planting in 7–10 days under cool wet conditions.
Tomato___Late_blight.time_sensitivity: ACT IMMEDIATELY. Apply systemic fungicide within 24 hours of first lesion. If the outbreak is regional, defensive fungicide programs are the only realistic path forward.
action_steps: Apply a systemic fungicide (cymoxanil, propamocarb, fluazinam) at first lesion — within 24 hours if possible.
Increase scouting to daily once the disease is in the area; check the USABlight network for regional alerts.
Destroy heavily infected plants by burying or bagging — do not compost.
Plant resistant cultivars (Defiant PhR, Mountain Magic, Iron Lady) in regions with regular late blight pressure.
Improve air circulation through aggressive pruning and wider spacing in subsequent plantings.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.summary: A fungal disease (Passalora fulva) primarily affecting greenhouse tomatoes; produces yellow patches on upper leaf surface and olive-green velvety growth on the underside.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.time_sensitivity: Apply protectant fungicide at first symptom; manage greenhouse humidity (target below 85%) continuously as the primary control.
action_steps: Reduce greenhouse humidity below 85% through ventilation, heating, and reduced misting.
Apply a protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper) at first symptom.
Increase plant spacing and aggressively prune lower leaves to improve air circulation.
Use resistant cultivars (most modern greenhouse varieties carry Cf-resistance genes).
Sanitize the greenhouse between crops — wash structures and remove all plant debris.

sources.name: Cornell Cooperative Extension — Tomato Leaf Mold
Penn State Extension — Tomato Leaf Mold

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.summary: A fungal disease (Septoria lycopersici) producing small circular spots with gray centers and dark borders; severe infections defoliate plants from the bottom up.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.time_sensitivity: Apply protectant fungicide at first symptom and continue every 7–14 days. Mulch application is the highest-leverage non-chemical step.
action_steps: Apply a protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) at first symptom; reapply every 7–14 days.
Mulch heavily with straw, leaves, or plastic to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves.
Stake or cage plants and aggressively prune lower leaves.
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base of the plant in the morning.
Rotate to a non-Solanaceae crop for at least 2 years.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.summary: A pest (Tetranychus urticae, not a disease) producing stippled yellow leaves and fine webbing on the underside; outbreaks accelerate in hot dry weather.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.time_sensitivity: Act at first detection — populations double every 2–3 days in warm dry weather. Biological control (predator mites) is the preferred long-term solution.
action_steps: Release commercial predator mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis or Neoseiulus californicus) at first detection — this is the highest-leverage long-term solution.
Spray plants with strong water jets to physically dislodge mites and increase humidity (mites thrive in dry conditions).
If chemical control is needed, use a miticide rotation (abamectin, bifenazate, spiromesifen) — avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill beneficials.
Avoid pyrethroids — they can cause spider mite outbreaks by killing predators and biological controls.
Maintain consistent irrigation — drought-stressed plants are more attractive to mites.

sources.name: UMN Extension — Spider Mites on Vegetables

Tomato___Target_Spot.summary: A fungal disease (Corynespora cassiicola) producing brown leaf spots with concentric rings and target-like fruit lesions; common in humid coastal regions.
Tomato___Target_Spot.time_sensitivity: Begin protectant fungicide at first symptom; continue every 7–14 days in humid weather. Resistance management (rotating fungicide groups) matters because the pathogen develops resistance quickly.
action_steps: Apply a protectant fungicide (chlorothalonil or mancozeb) at first symptom; reapply every 7–14 days.
Rotate fungicide modes of action — Corynespora develops resistance quickly to single-site fungicides (strobilurins, SDHIs).
Maintain canopy openness through pruning and adequate plant spacing.
Use drip irrigation to keep leaves dry.
Rotate to a non-host crop for at least 2 years after severe outbreaks.

sources.name: NC State Extension — Tomato Disease Guide

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.display_name: Tomato — Tomato yellow leaf curl virus
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.summary: A viral disease spread by whiteflies (Bemisia tabaci); causes upward-curling chlorotic leaves, severe stunting, and near-total yield loss in young infected plants.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.time_sensitivity: There is no curative treatment. Focus on whitefly vector control from transplant and removal of infected plants to protect neighbors.
action_steps: Plant resistant varieties (Tygress, Defiant, several TY-gene varieties) in regions with consistent whitefly pressure.
Control whitefly populations with reflective mulch, yellow sticky traps, and selective insecticides (cyantraniliprole, flupyradifurone) under integrated pest management.
Remove and destroy heavily infected plants immediately — leaving them in the field provides reservoir for vector transmission.
Cover transplants with row cover until flowering to physically exclude whiteflies.
Plant a trap crop (squash or eggplant) at field edges to draw whiteflies away from the tomato crop.

sources.name: UF/IFAS — Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.summary: A viral disease (ToMV) producing mottled light/dark green leaves, distorted growth, and reduced yield; spreads mechanically through tools, hands, and tobacco products.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.time_sensitivity: There is no curative treatment. Sanitation and resistant varieties are the only effective controls. Remove infected plants immediately.
action_steps: Plant resistant varieties (most modern hybrids carry the Tm-1, Tm-2, or Tm-22 resistance genes).
Sanitize hands, tools, and stakes with 10% bleach or non-fat dry milk between plants when working in the planting.
Do not use tobacco products in the planting — tobacco mosaic virus and tomato mosaic virus are closely related and tobacco is a primary reservoir.
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately; bag them rather than composting.
Rotate to a non-host crop for at least 2 years after a severe outbreak.

sources.name: Penn State Extension — Tomato Mosaic Virus

Tomato___healthy.summary: No disease detected. Leaf shows healthy tissue characteristic of a well-managed tomato plant.
maintenance_tips: Scout weekly throughout the season — tomatoes are susceptible to ~15 common diseases plus several major pests.
Use drip irrigation and mulch with straw or plastic to keep leaves dry and prevent soil splash.
Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground.
Rotate planting locations every year if possible; never plant tomatoes where any Solanaceae crop grew in the previous 2 years.
